chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Senior Capstone Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Development of system integration skills for solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software project that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and teamwork. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

* Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.
* Design, implement and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of computing requirements in the context of the program's discipline.
* Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
* Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgements in computing practice based on legal and ethical principles.
* Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the program's discipline.
* Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals to produce computing-based solutions.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: UCRT - University Required Oral Communication

chunks.title: Communication Requirements for C Credit
chunks.content:
* To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course communication requirement below:
* A minimum of 35% of your final grade is based on written and oral communication assignments.
* Of this communication component, at least 70% is based on individually written work and individual public speaking; group work does not count toward this standard.
* To receive C credit, students must pass both the written and public speaking components of the course. Failure to pass either component will result in failing the course, regardless of performance on other assignments.
* Each student will produce at least 1,250 words of individually written work and deliver a minimum of 5 minutes of individual public speaking.
* Assignments fulfilling this requirement include individual progress reports, project proposals, final technical documentation, and individual presentations or demonstrations.
* Resumes and related materials are accepted only if specific to the major; take-home essay exams may count if they allow drafting and revision. Oral presentation slides, scripts, web sites, and posters count when accompanied by individual presentations.
No more than 10% of the communication grade may be assessed by undergraduate aides, and all grading by aides is supervised by the instructor. Failure to meet the individual written or oral communication requirements will result in failure of the course, regardless of overall numerical average.

chunks.title: Grading Policy and Components
chunks.content:
Your grade for this course reflects your mastery of course material and is determined by multiple components. As a communications course, you must pass the communications components with a C or better to earn a passing grade in the course. Failure to pass the communications components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

Specific grading practices will be announced when assignments are given through assignment descriptions and assignment rubrics. When a rubric is given, the work must meet the condition of Minimum Viable Product (MVP)-quality before being assessed by the rubric.

* MVP-quality is defined in this context as the work must be at least non-trivially complete, of appropriate quality, and demonstrative of genuine effort.
Each graded assignment in this course falls under one of the following assignment categories:

* Team Artifacts
  * Project Artifacts: Your team will work on
* Team Communications
  * Weekly Updates: Your team will provide a weekly report of your project updates as preparation for weekly team meetings.
  * Project Report: Your team will provide a full project report that documents the entire project through periodic section milestones.
  * Poster Materials: Your team will design presentation posters for use in scheduled campus poster presentation events.
* Individual Communications
  * Oral Communications: You will be assessed on your oral communications from presentation activities.

chunks.title: Grading Calculation and EMRN Scale
chunks.content:
* Written Documents: You will be assessed on your writing performance from your document submissions.
* End-of-Semester Deliverables
  * Final Presentations: Your team will communicate end-of-semester presentation activities.
  * Final Materials: Your team will provide final project materials.
* Assessment Elements
  * Peer Evaluations: You will have multiple opportunities to submit peer evaluations and corresponding reflections that assess your teammates and yourself, respectively, on the team project.
  * Individual Performances: Your individual performance to the success of the team project will be graded. In any team environment, striving to be a valuable member of your team is critical. Assessment sources such as logged code commits, peer evaluations, attendance, participation, retrospectives, confidential remarks to the instructor, and instructor/TA observations will be evaluated for this grade.
* Ethics Training
  * Ethics Sessions: You will review online materials on ethics that will be assessed through quizzes.

Grading Calculation
Works will be calculated with the specifications grading approach. Each work will be evaluated to determine if it meets the specifications. Calculations will be based on the specification grading's EMRN scale:

* [S] Satisfactory (i.e., Meets Expectations): Understanding of the concepts is evident through correct work and clear, audience-appropriate explanations. Some revision or expansion is needed, but no significant gaps or errors are present. No additional instruction on the concepts is needed.
* [U] Unsatisfactory (i.e., Revision Needed): Partial understanding of the concepts is evident, but there are significant gaps that remain. Needs further work, more review, and/or improved explanations.
* [U-] Highly Unsatisfactory (i.e., Not Assessable): Not enough information is present in the work to determine whether there is understanding of the concepts. The work is fragmentary or contains significant omissions. Or there are too many issues to justify correcting each one.

chunks.title: Grading Scale and Assignment Category Scores
chunks.content:
For assignments hosted on their respective platforms (e.g., Canvas), the EMRN scale is mapped numerically as follows:

* [S+]: Highly Satisfactory as 1 ½ points.
* [S]: Satisfactory as 1 point.
* [U]: Unsatisfactory as ½ point.
* [U-]: Highly Unsatisfactory as 0 points.

Grading Scale
Works will be assigned scores that fall under one of two level:

* [S*] Satisfactory Type: a satisfactory score [S] or a highly satisfactory score [S+]
* [U*] Unsatisfactory Type: an unsatisfactory score [U] or a highly unsatisfactory score [U-]

The following table shows the maximum allowable number of [U*] scores for each assignment category per letter grade. Note that assignment category letter grades are therefore calculated by the number of [U*] scores—not by the average of assignment scores—in that category.

| Assignment Element | Assignment Category | A | B | C | D | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Team Artifacts | Project Artifacts | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Team Communications | Weekly Updates | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Team Communications | Project Report | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Team Communications | Poster Materials | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Individual Communications | Oral Communications | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Individual Communications | Written Documents | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| End-of-Semester Deliverables | Final Presentations | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| End-of-Semester Deliverables | Final Materials | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Assessment Elements | Peer Evaluations | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |
| Assessment Elements | Individual Performances | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |

chunks.title: Submission, Resubmission, and Redemption of Assignments
chunks.content:
| Ethics Training | Ethics Sessions | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4+ |

Your overall course letter grade will be based on the lowest letter grade of all the assignment categories. These letter grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's grading system):

* A = excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing

Submission of Assignments
All works must be submitted electronically—either directly through Canvas or indirectly through Canvas-connected external tools (e.g., Peerceptiv).
Any works submitted non-electronically or through message-based forms—such as email, Canvas Inbox, or an assignment's comment section—are not accepted for submission.

Resubmission of Assignments
Assignments are eligible for resubmission of work that was initially scored as [U]:

* The exceptions are assignments from the Assessment Elements category, since those assignments are time-sensitive.

The conditions for resubmission are as follows:

* previous submission is MVP-quality
* resubmission fully addresses the work's feedback
* resubmission includes an explanatory remark in the assignment's comment section of what specifically was unsatisfactory and how it was addressed
* resubmission occurs up to one week after the initial deadlines
* only one resubmission is guaranteed to be allowed per assignment by default
* any additional resubmission is regraded at the discretion of the instructor

Redemption of Assignments
Assignment redemptions are opportunities for works with [U*] scores to be redeemed as [S] by other works with [S*] scores in the same assignment category.

chunks.title: Redemption Eligibility
chunks.content:
Only the following assignment category is eligible for redemption of work that was initially scored as [U*]:

* Assessment Elements: Individual Performances
  * Each work with a [U] score can be redeemed from one subsequent work with an [S+] score.
  * Each work with a [U-] score can be redeemed from two subsequent works with [S+] scores.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy
Work submitted from assignments are generally accepted as late work without penalty up to two days after their posted deadline. The exceptions are work submitted for the following assignment category:

* Assessment Elements: Peer Evaluations

The added condition for the late work to be accepted is that it is of Minimally Viable Product (MVP)-quality as defined in the Grading Policy section.
Any work that is either not from assignments of eligible categories or is below MVP-quality will not be accepted as late work.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weeks 1–4: Project Framing and Early Design
Students are introduced to senior capstone expectations and industry-sponsored and faculty-proposed projects. Activities focus on identifying and formulating a computing problem, understanding domain context and prior work, functioning effectively in teams, ethical considerations, defining project goals, requirements, and success criteria, and establishing project management foundations.
Assessment emphasizes iterative progress on early design, planning, and communication deliverables.

Weeks 5–7: Design Exploration and Evaluation Planning
Teams develop system designs derived from defined requirements and constraints. Students learn to create evaluation plans aligned with their definition of success and prepare for implementation through targeted skill development.
Assessment emphasizes progress on design, documentation, planning, and evaluation preparation.

Weeks 8–11: Implementation and Evaluation
Teams implement their systems and continue iterative evaluation, including testing and review. User interface design and technical design decisions are communicated and refined. User evaluation planning and execution begin during this phase.
Assessment emphasizes implementation progress, evaluation activities, and technical documentation.

Weeks 12–14: System Refinement and Presentation Preparation
Teams complete implementation and evaluation, analyze results, and determine implications. Students prepare and deliver in-class and external presentations and demonstrations.
Assessment emphasizes system quality, evaluation results, communication, and professionalism.

Weeks 15–16: Final Deliverables and Review
Teams finalize all project deliverables and participate in final presentations, demonstrations, and reviews with industry or instructional staff.
Final assessment considers project completion, documentation, evaluation results, and professional communication.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools—such as ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, and others—are rapidly changing the practice of software engineering. In this course, you will be both required and encouraged to use AI in structured, intentional ways to deepen your understanding of how these tools affect your workflow and outputs. The course policy below is intended to help you learn how to use modern AI tools responsibly and ethically in software engineering.

Required Uses of AI
Certain assignments will direct you to use AI tools for specific tasks (e.g., generating code comments, comparing your code to AI-generated code, or improving writing). Follow all instructions for these assignments precisely, including any prompt engineering or documentation requirements.

Permitted Uses of AI
AI may be used to assist with limited, well-defined tasks (such as debugging or suggesting code comments), but may not be used to design your entire project or to complete assignments whose primary learning outcomes involve your own design and implementation.
Direct use of AI to generate complete solutions for programming assignments, project designs, or other major tasks is prohibited unless explicitly stated otherwise in the assignment instructions.

AI and Academic Integrity Requirements
For any usage of AI in any part of your work—including explicit assignment directions—refer to the following points in order to maintain academic integrity:

* Clearly cite any AI-generated content by listing each tool, document prompts, and output, as well as explaining how the content was created.
* Properly attribute AI-generated material, since failure to do so is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office.
* Limit usage of no more than 30% of any assignment to AI-generated content.

chunks.title: Technology Support
chunks.content:
Technology Services (IT) - Main Campus
Hours: 24/7
Phone: (979) 845-8300
Email: helpdesk@tamu.edu
Call/Chat/Email/visit: https://it.tamu.edu/help

Canvas LMS Technical Support
Hours: 24/7/365
Phone: (877) 354-4821
Email: support@instructure.com
Support is available by clicking the Help button at the far left in the Canvas global navigation menu.
Canvas Resources are also linked on the home page of every Canvas course.

Learning Resources
In addition to office hours with the instructor and TAs, you have access to many academic supports as a student. These supports are intended for you to achieve the academic success you are truly capable of. Most students access academic support at some point while pursuing their degree. Here are a few that are most relevant to this course.

Peer Teacher Central
Peer Teacher Central (PTC) provides a group of students you can talk to for help in 100, 200, and 300 level CSCE courses. Sometimes it is more comfortable talking to a fellow student instead of the instructor or TA. You can get help from students who have successfully completed this course in person on the first floor of the Peterson building in Room 127 or after hours on their discord server. You will also have access to peer teachers in the lab. Students consistently rate peer teachers as having a positive impact on their academic performance.

chunks.title: University Writing Center
chunks.content: Writing and speaking are challenging. The University Writing Center has trained peer consultants available to work with you on any kind of writing or speaking project, including research papers, lab reports, application essays, or creative writing, and at any stage of your process, whether you're deciding on a topic or reviewing your final draft. You can also get help with public speaking, presentations, and group projects. You can schedule in-person and Zoom appointments, their writing and speaking guides for additional self-study, or email uwc@tamu.edu for help.

chunks.title: University Policies
chunks.content: This section outlines the university-level policies that must be included in each course syllabus. The TAMU Faculty Advisory Council established the wording of these policies.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Attendance plays an important role in this class. Lack of attendance in class activities such as lectures and in-class meetings can harm the rest of the team's efforts in the project.
Incidents of absences and lateness during class days where attendance is expected will affect individual performance scoring when those incidents occur, unless those incidents are approved by the instructor in advance.

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
If you must miss a class or lab for a legitimate and documented reason (see Student Rule 7), then submit valid absence documentation to the applicable assignment on Canvas by navigating to: Modules → Student Resources → Valid Absence Request Documentation.

* Note: Documentation will not be accepted by email.
